figures.caption: Fig 8. User interface of the application (MultiEcoCI) used to model multilocus ecological genotypes and the invasion dynamics of CIs. In this example, the application models a sympatric population with 80% of the habitat resources allocated to pure parental ecotypes (equally divided between niches 𝐴𝐴 and 𝐵𝐵) and 20% allocated equally among hybrid niches to sustain viable hybrid ecotypes. The initial genotype and habitat resource distributions used are shown in Fig 9. Each individual carries three ecological gene loci (specified by the number of digits in the CI vector, which is three in this example) and one mating-bias locus. The Invasion Point plot option displays all initial 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 population ratios in the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 phase portrait against the invading CI population ratio (which is 0.01 in this example). The figure illustrates the invasion dynamics of a chromosomal inversion (CI = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0𝑋𝑋) that captures two of the three locally adaptive ecological alleles in niche 𝐴𝐴 and the 𝑋𝑋 mating-bias allele. Before the CI invasion, the initial population ratios of the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁 and 𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 ecotypes (blue dots) in the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 phase portrait converge to a fixed point at 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 = 0.8606/ 0.1428 (green dot) after 1000 generations (𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐺𝐺). Afterward, when the CI is introduced at an initial population ratio of 0.01, it is able to successfully invade niche 𝐴𝐴 and stabilize at a population ratio of 0.9877 after 200 generations (𝑃𝑃𝑜𝑜𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐺𝐺). The CI invasion plot on the right shows this invasion trajectory. This invasion shifts the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 fixed point to 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 = 0.9920/0.0161 (red dot), located closer to the lower-right corner of the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 phase portrait, indicating stronger premating RI. Consequently, the total inter-niche offspring ratio (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶) and maladaptive hybrid loss (𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻) progressively decline from the 𝐶𝐶𝑛𝑛𝐼𝐼𝑃𝑃𝐼𝐼𝑎𝑎𝐼𝐼 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 to 𝐹𝐹𝐼𝐼𝑛𝑛𝑎𝑎𝐼𝐼 𝑃𝑃𝑜𝑜𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 stages (i.e., from 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶/𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 = 0.67894/ 0.27424 at 𝐶𝐶𝑛𝑛𝐼𝐼𝑃𝑃𝐼𝐼𝑎𝑎𝐼𝐼 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 to 0.59204/0.20618 at 𝐹𝐹𝐼𝐼𝑛𝑛𝑎𝑎𝐼𝐼 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶, and finally reaching 0.51302/0.09656 at 𝐹𝐹𝐼𝐼𝑛𝑛𝑎𝑎𝐼𝐼 𝑃𝑃𝑜𝑜𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 after the CI invasion), producing ever stronger RI between the parental niche ecotypes. See text for a description of the parametric variables used in the interface.

Fig 17. Single-line plots showing how the presence of mating-bias hybrids decreases RI, whereas the invasion of a CI capturing high mating-bias alleles increases RI. When the Single Line plot option is selected in Fig 14, the 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 phase portrait plots the single-line vector trajectory of the initial genotype ratio (𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 = 1/0, as specified in Fig 16) across the pre-CI and post-CI phases of the simulation. Similarly, the 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 phase portrait plots the single-line invasion trajectory of the CI mutation (i.e., the 𝐾𝐾 mutant allele in niche 𝐴𝐴). Displayed at the bottom of the figure are the 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 and 𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃 values at different stages of the simulation. The variable 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 (total inter-niche offspring) is defined as in Fig 10 and serves as a measure of inter-niche mating and premating RI. The variable 𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃 represents the total ratio of ecological hybrid offspring produced from inter-niche matings. Because ecological hybrids are not viable in this model, 𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃 is equivalent to 𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 in Fig 10 and reflects the degree of ecological hybrid loss that drives the evolution of RI between niche ecotypes. As shown, 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 and 𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃 increase after the pre-CI phase because the emergence of viable mating-bias hybrid genotypes reduces the effective mating bias and weakens sexual selection, thereby diminishing premating RI. In the post-CI phase, however, the invasion of the adaptive CI (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁0) reduces the effective number of mating-bias loci and limits the production of mating-bias hybrids. This shifts the final preCI 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 fixed point (green dot) toward the lower-right corner of the phase portrait (red dot), resulting in stronger RI between niche-𝐴𝐴 and niche-𝐵𝐵 ecotypes.

Fig 55. In a multilocus incompatibility model, strengthening disruptive ecological selection (decreasing 𝒇𝒇) promotes fixed-point convergence in the lower-right quadrant of the 𝑨𝑨𝑷𝑷/𝑩𝑩𝑷𝑷 phase portrait. As shown, reducing f from 0.1 to 0.07 in Fig 53a generates a fixed point in lower-right quadrant of the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait. This occurs because lower 𝑓𝑓 reduces gene flow from inter-niche matings, allowing the locally prevalent incompatibility allele (𝑃𝑃 or 𝑄𝑄) in each niche to more effectively eliminate the less prevalent allele. With all other parameters held constant, fixed-point convergence occurs when 𝑓𝑓 ≤ 0.07.

Fig 56. In a multilocus incompatibility model with a stable fixed point in the 𝑨𝑨𝑷𝑷/𝑩𝑩𝑷𝑷 phase portrait, increasing the number of incompatibility loci shifts the fixed point closer to the lower-right corner and strengthens postzygotic RI. As shown, increasing the number of loci in Fig 54a from three to four moves the fixed point toward the lower-right corner of the phase portrait, resulting in stronger postzygotic RI between niche ecotypes.

Fig 20. 𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨/𝑩𝑩𝑨𝑨 phase-portrait dynamics under divergent incompatibility alleles. The phase portrait shows that when ecotype-𝐴𝐴 and ecotype-𝐵𝐵 populations already carry high frequencies of different incompatibility alleles (𝑃𝑃 and 𝑄𝑄, respectively), initial 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 population ratios within the lower-right quadrant converge to a stable fixed point. For the specified parameter values, disruptive ecological selection can drive 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 ratios that start farther from the lower-right corner toward a fixed point closer to it, thereby strengthening postzygotic RI between niche ecotypes. In contrast, when 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 ratios are already near the lower-right corner, gene flow can shift the equilibrium to a fixed point farther from this region, resulting in weaker postzygotic RI.

Fig 30b. In a system with three-locus ecological genotypes, when 𝑵𝑵𝑨𝑨 < 𝑵𝑵𝑩𝑩, the invasion of a CI capturing a partial set of adaptive ecological alleles and the prevalent mating-bias allele 𝑿𝑿 in niche 𝑨𝑨 strengthens RI. When the habitat 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴:𝑁𝑁𝐵𝐵 ratios in Fig 30a are reversed (i.e., 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴:𝑁𝑁𝐵𝐵 = 0.2: 0.6) so that 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 < 𝑁𝑁𝐵𝐵, with the hybrid distribution unchanged, successful invasion of the CI (CI = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0𝑋𝑋) restores hybrid offspring production and disruptive ecological selection, leading to stronger premating RI through reinforcement. As shown, the CI invasion shifts the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 fixed point closer to the lower-right corner of the phase portrait, resulting in stronger premating RI. Both 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 and 𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 decrease after the CI invasion.

Fig 12. Bar graph showing the genotype compositions across all ecological niches after the pre-CI phase in a model with three-locus ecological genotypes. Selecting the Pre-CI Distribution tab at the top of the user interface in Fig 10 displays the genotype compositions in all ecological niches after 1000 pre-CI generations, prior to the invasion of the CI.

Fig 11. Bar graph displaying the user-specified habitat resource distribution in a model with three-locus ecological genotypes. Selecting the Initial Distribution tab at the top of the user interface in Fig 10 displays the specified niche resource distribution. In this example, the “80%” habitat resource option allocates 80% of the total niche resources equally between the parental niches 𝐴𝐴 and 𝐵𝐵, while the remaining 20% are divided equally among all hybrid niches.

Fig 10. Single-line plots showing an 𝑿𝑿 mutant matingbias allele invading niche 𝑨𝑨 and reaching a fixed-point polymorphism before an adaptive CI subsequently invades and further strengthens premating RI. In Fig 8, selecting the Single Line plot option displays the invasion trajectory of a high–mating-bias 𝑋𝑋 mutant allele in niche 𝐴𝐴 during the pre-CI phase, followed by the invasion trajectory of the CI mutant (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0𝑋𝑋) during the post-CI phase. In the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 phase portrait, the initial 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁 population ratio (𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 = 0.01/0) is shown as a blue dot. It reaches a fixedpoint polymorphism after 1000 generations, marked by the green dot. The subsequent invasion of the CI shifts this fixed point closer to the lower-right corner, marked by the red dot, indicating stronger premating RI.

Fig 61. Schematic diagram illustrating the interactions among premating barriers, postmating barriers, and ecological divergence. Premating RI may result from mating-bias barriers or habitat-preference barriers. Postmating RI may be either extrinsic, driven by disruptive ecological selection, or intrinsic, caused by postzygotic hybrid incompatibilities. Reciprocal arrows indicate mutual reinforcement among these processes, forming a positive feedback loop that strengthens the interacting mechanisms.

Fig 33. In a system with three-locus ecological genotypes, the invasion fitness of a CI decreases when it captures locally maladaptive ecological alleles. If the CI in Fig 31a (CI = 𝑎𝑎00𝑋𝑋) captures a locally maladaptive allele (𝑏𝑏) in niche 𝐴𝐴 (CI = 𝑎𝑎0𝑏𝑏𝑋𝑋), its fitness is reduced when nonrandom assortment of ecological and mating-bias alleles has been established across niches after the pre-CI phase. As a result, the CI has lower fitness than its same-niche counterparts in niche 𝐴𝐴 and cannot invade.

Fig 32. In a system with three-locus ecological genotypes, a CI mutant capturing two adaptive ecological alleles together with a high-mating-bias allele in niche 𝑨𝑨 forms a magic trait with synergistically increased invasion fitness, even when the mating bias is weak. With all other parameters held constant, the pre-CI generation in Fig 31d is set to one, and the CI vector is specified as 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0𝑋𝑋 to simulate the invasion dynamics of a CI mutant capturing a partial set (2 of 3) of adaptive ecological alleles and the 𝑋𝑋 mating-bias allele in a sympatric population carrying only the 𝑌𝑌 allele. This CI mutant acts as a magic trait, exhibiting a synergistic fitness advantage that enables invasion despite the weak mating bias (𝛼𝛼 = 0.6). Without linkage to the adaptive ecological alleles, the 𝑋𝑋 allele alone cannot invade; however, when captured within the CI, it can hitchhike to near fixation in niche 𝐴𝐴. The time (𝐶𝐶₉₅) for the mutant CI (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0𝑋𝑋) to reach 95% of its steady-state frequency is 28 generations— shorter than the 𝐶𝐶₉₅ observed when the CI captures only one ecological allele (60 generations for 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 𝑎𝑎00𝑋𝑋 in Fig 31d)—indicating that capturing more adaptive ecological alleles confers greater invasion fitness.

Fig 39b. Post-CI Distribution bar graph showing the genotype distribution following the invasion of an adaptive CI mutant (𝑪𝑪𝑪𝑪 = 𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨) in a system with three mating-bias loci. The bar graph displays the genotype distribution after the CI invasion shown in Fig 39a. As illustrated, the CI reduces the effective number of matingbias loci from three to one and suppresses the production of intermediate mating-bias hybrids, leading to premating RI, as reflected by reduced post-CI 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 and 𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃 values.

Fig 14. User interface of the application (MultiSexCI) used to model multilocus mating-bias genotypes and the invasion dynamics of CIs that capture mating-bias alleles. The application models a sympatric population with multilocus mating-bias genotypes. For ecological selection, it adopts the same framework as the model without viable ecological hybrids shown in Fig 3. The variables 𝑓𝑓 (offspring return ratio), 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 (normalized niche 𝐴𝐴 carrying capacity), and 𝑛𝑛 (number of matching rounds) are defined as in Fig 3. For sexual selection, the number of letters in the 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝑣𝑣𝑃𝑃𝑣𝑣𝑃𝑃𝑜𝑜𝑃𝑃 specifies the number of gene loci in the mating-bias genotypes. Each locus carries either the 𝑋𝑋 or 𝑌𝑌 allele, and the CI captures either the 𝑋𝑋 or 𝑌𝑌 allele—or “0” to indicate no capture. The variable 𝛼𝛼 specifies the maximum mating bias between the pure genotypes (e.g., 𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁 and 𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁 in this three-locus example). Mating biases between different genotypes are determined by their genetic distances and are listed in the Matching Compatibility Table (Fig 15). The variable 𝑓𝑓𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 specifies the offspring return ratio of the CI. 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴 and 𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 are the initial CI mutant population ratios in niches 𝐴𝐴 and 𝐵𝐵, respectively. 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐺𝐺 and 𝑃𝑃𝑜𝑜𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐺𝐺 specify the number of generations simulated in the pre-CI and post-CI phases. Selecting the Manual option in Initial Genotype Distribution opens the table in Fig 16, allowing users to define the initial genotype distribution before the pre-CI phase begins. The figure displays the Invasion Point plot option. In the 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 phase portrait, 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋 is calculated as the normalized ratio of the pure 𝑋𝑋 genotype (𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁) in niche 𝐴𝐴 relative to the total of pure 𝑋𝑋 and 𝑌𝑌 genotypes in that niche, excluding all hybrid genotypes (Eq. 1). 𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 is calculated in the same way for niche 𝐵𝐵 (Eq. 2). Given the specified parameters, the 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 phase portrait shows all initial population ratios in the vector field (blue dots) converging to fixed points (green dots) during the pre-CI phase. At the start of the post-CI phase, a CI mutant (CI = 𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁0), represented by allele 𝐾𝐾 with an initial population ratio of 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴 = 0.01, successfully invades and reaches a fixed-point polymorphism (red dot at 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 = 0.9125/0.1412) in the 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 phase portrait. Consequently, the final pre-CI 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 fixed point in niche 𝐴𝐴 (green dot at 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 = 0.9407/0.3086) shifts toward the lower-right corner (red dot at 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 = 0.9416/0.1014), indicating stronger RI between the niche-𝐴𝐴 and niche-𝐵𝐵 ecotypes. The pre-CI 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 phase portrait shows an invasion-resistant pattern.
Fig 1. Flowchart showing the life cycle of a sympatric population used in the computer simulations.

Fig 38. Post-CI Distribution bar graph showing that an adaptive CI (𝑪𝑪𝑪𝑪 = 𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨) cannot invade when 𝜶𝜶 = 𝒂𝒂 and no mating-bias hybrids exist in a system with three mating-bias loci. In Fig 36g, setting 𝛼𝛼 = 0 eliminates all mating-bias hybrids at the 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 fixed point. When 𝛼𝛼 = 0, an adaptive CI mutant (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁) cannot invade because its invasion fitness depends on reducing the production of mating-bias hybrid offspring—by lowering the effective number of mating-bias loci experienced by the system—and thereby increasing premating RI to reduce maladaptive ecological hybrid loss. When 𝛼𝛼 = 0 and 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶/𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃 = 0/0, maximum mating bias has already been achieved, which leaves no room for a CI to further enhance it.

Fig 37b. Decreasing the size of niche 𝑨𝑨 (𝑵𝑵𝑨𝑨) reduces the invasion-resistant region along the 𝑨𝑨-axis in the 𝑨𝑨𝑿𝑿/𝑩𝑩𝑿𝑿 phase portrait of a system with three mating-bias loci. When the niche-𝐴𝐴 size in Fig 36c is reduced from 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 = 0.6 to 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 = 0.4, while keeping all other parametric values unchanged, the invasion-resistant region contracts away from the 𝑁𝑁-axis. This creates a wedge-shaped area lying along the 𝑁𝑁-axis and pointing toward the origin, within which invasion is possible for initial 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 population ratios falling inside this region. As 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 is further reduced toward 0.3, the wedge-shaped region becomes increasingly compressed toward the 𝑁𝑁-axis, and the fixed point shifts closer to the 𝑁𝑁-axis and farther from the lower-right corner of the phase portrait. When 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 ≤ 0.3, the wedge-shaped region disappears entirely, and the 𝐴𝐴𝑋𝑋/𝐵𝐵𝑋𝑋 phase portrait becomes divergent. Similarly, increasing 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 greater than 0.5 produces a comparable effect along the 𝑁𝑁-axis, and the phase portrait becomes globally divergent once 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 ≥ 0.7.

Fig 24a. In a system with four-locus ecological genotypes, the invasion of a CI that captures only a partial set of locally adaptive ecological alleles—but not a mating-bias allele—partially restores heterokaryotype hybrid loss but still leads to weakened premating RI. In contrast to the example in Figs 23a and 23b, where the CI captures all niche-𝐴𝐴 alleles, the CI here captures only two of the four ecological alleles (CI = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎00), resulting in partial restoration of heterokaryotype hybrid loss. However, its invasion still produces a weakened premating RI, shifting the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 fixed point away from the lower-right corner of the phase portrait (from 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 = 0.9364/0.0636 to 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 = 0.8809/0.1192). Consequently, 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 increases from 0.54875 to 0.5800 after the post-CI phase of the simulation, indicating increased inter-niche mating. Because this CI captures only a subset of adaptive ecological alleles, its invasion fitness is reduced relative to that of a CI capturing all ecological alleles. This is reflected in the invasion plot, where the CI invades more slowly and ultimately stabilizes at a lower frequency ratio in niche 𝐴𝐴 than in the invasion plots shown in Figs 23a and 23b.

Fig 59. The presence of CIs capturing extreme-type incompatibility alleles reduces the effective number of incompatibility gene loci and impedes convergence to fixed points and the establishment of postzygotic RI. The parameter values used in Fig 54a permit the emergence of a fixed point in the lower-right quadrant of the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait in the absence of CIs, but this fixed point fails to form when a CI capturing extreme-𝑃𝑃 alleles is present in the initial population. As shown in the Invasion Point plot, when setting 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃-𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐺𝐺 to 1, the CI mutant (CI = 𝑝𝑝𝑝𝑝0, 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴 = 0.01) in the population rapidly invades, reduces the effective number of incompatibility gene loci experienced by individuals in the system, and prevents convergence to a fixed point in the lower-right quadrant of the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait.

Fig 58. No CIs capturing incompatibility alleles can invade when no fixed point exists in the pre-CI 𝑨𝑨𝑷𝑷/𝑩𝑩𝑷𝑷 phase portrait. Increasing 𝑣𝑣 in Figs 57a–57f from 0.12 to 0.3 causes the pre-CI 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait to become divergent and eliminates the fixed point. Consequently, no CI capturing any number or combination of incompatibility alleles is able to invade. Therefore, invasion of an adaptive CI is possible only when a fixed point already exists in the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait.

Fig 49b. An existing postzygotic barrier increases the effective strength of selection against inter-niche hybrids, enabling the emergence of a second, weaker postzygotic barrier. When the viability ratio for the second postzygotic barrier in Fig 49a is increased from 𝑣𝑣2 = 0.22 to 𝑣𝑣2 = 0.5, the presence of the first postzygotic barrier still allows the second barrier to establish a stable fixed point in the 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝2/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝2 phase portrait. In contrast, a weak postzygotic barrier with 𝑣𝑣2 = 0.5 cannot produce fixed-point convergence when acting alone. By increasing the effective strength of disruptive selection against inter-niche hybrid offspring, the first postzygotic barrier relaxes the conditions required for establishment of the second postzygotic barrier.

Fig 13. Bar graph showing the genotype compositions across all ecological niches after the post-CI phase in a model with three-locus ecological genotypes. Selecting the Post-CI Distribution tab at the top of the user interface in Fig 10 displays the genotype compositions across all ecological niches after 200 post-CI generations. The black bars in the graph represent the ratios of the CI in each niche, while the blue and red bars indicate the ratios of the 𝑋𝑋 and 𝑌𝑌 alleles in the uninverted niche genotypes. As shown, the CI (𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0𝑋𝑋) is able to invade niches 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎 and 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑏𝑏 and attain high ratios in a polymorphism. The CI does not reach fixation, however, because mating among viable hybrid populations continues to regenerate the uninverted niche 𝐴𝐴 ecotype.
Fig 6. Unit Offspring Matrix (𝑼𝑼).
Fig 2. Flowchart showing the algorithm used to match eligible mating individuals and reproduce offspring.
Fig 3. Mathematical model of a 2-mating-bias-allele, 2-ecological-niche sympatric ecosystem.

Fig 48b. Positive feedback between premating habitatpreference barriers and postzygotic incompatibility barriers strengthens both barriers and increases overall RI. In the GUI display, the hybrid-incompatibility barrier in Fig 47a first establishes postzygotic RI by generating a stable fixed point at 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 = 0.97617/0.14938. Consequently, reinforcement facilitates the emergence of the premating habitat-preference barrier shown in Fig 48a. In the 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 phase portrait, a mutant 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴 allele is able to invade at the established 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 fixed-point population and reach a stable fixed-point polymorphism to produce premating RI. Phase-portrait analyses show that premating habitatpreference isolation strengthens the postzygotic barrier by shifting its fixed point toward the lower-right corner of the 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 phase portrait (from 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 = 0.97617/0.14938 to 0.99651/0.00561), thereby increasing postzygotic RI between niche ecotypes. In turn, the strengthened postzygotic isolation further increases the premating habitat-preference barrier via classical reinforcement, shifting the 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 fixed point toward the lower-right corner of the 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 phase portrait (from 𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴/𝐵𝐵𝐴𝐴 = 0.83333/0.16667 to 0.97511/ 0.02566). Together, premating and postzygotic barriers mutually reinforce one another through positive feedback, resulting in stronger overall RI.

Fig 25. In a system with four-locus ecological genotypes, a CI capturing only one adaptive ecological allele—but not a mating-bias allele—has no effect on hybrid loss or pre-established RI. In the Fig 24a example, if the CI instead captures only a single locally adaptive allele in niche 𝐴𝐴 (CI = 𝑎𝑎000) without capturing a mating-bias allele, it has no effect on the existing hybrid loss or the established premating RI. The CI has no fitness advantage over same-niche individuals lacking the inversion and therefore cannot invade.

Fig 26. In a system with three-locus ecological genotypes, invasion of a CI capturing all adaptive ecological alleles and the prevalent mating-bias allele in niche 𝑨𝑨 nonetheless causes the system to become divergent. In this example, the CI captures all locally adaptive ecological alleles and the dominant 𝑋𝑋 mating-bias allele in niche 𝐴𝐴 (CI = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑋𝑋). The CI invades successfully and rises to near fixation in niche 𝐴𝐴. However, because no hybrid offspring are produced in inter-niche heterokaryotype matings between niche-𝐴𝐴 ecotypes carrying the CI and niche-𝐵𝐵 ecotypes, the effective value of 𝑓𝑓 = 0.5, and there is no maladaptive hybrid loss to drive or maintain the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 mating-bias barrier. Consequently, the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 phase portrait becomes divergent, and the 𝑌𝑌 mating-bias allele is eliminated, while the 𝑋𝑋 allele cannot be eliminated because it is linked with the niche-𝐴𝐴 ecotype through the CI.

Fig 52b. Invasion of a CI capturing one of the three locally adaptive ecological alleles and the locally prevalent incompatibility allele (𝑪𝑪𝑪𝑪 = 𝒂𝒂𝒂𝒂𝒂𝒂𝑷𝑷) shifts the 𝑨𝑨𝑨𝑨/𝑩𝑩𝑨𝑨 fixed point to the lower-right corner of the phase portrait but exhibits reduced invasion fitness. When the CI shown in Fig 52a captures only one locally adaptive ecological allele (CI = 𝑎𝑎00𝑃𝑃), its invasion at the 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 fixed point established in Fig 50a similarly displaces the fixed point to the lower-right corner of the phase portrait. However, relative to a CI capturing more locally adaptive ecological alleles (Fig 52a), this CI has reduced invasion fitness, as indicated by the longer time required to reach fixation in niche 𝐴𝐴 (~200 generations versus ~100 generations).

Fig 51. Invasion of a CI capturing only locally adaptive ecological alleles reduces disruptive ecological selection and weakens postzygotic reproductive isolation. The Invasion Point plot shows the successful invasion of a CI capturing two of three locally adaptive ecological alleles in niche 𝐴𝐴 (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎0) at the fixed point established in Fig 50a. As the CI mutant (initial invasion ratio = 0.01) successfully invades and becomes fixed in niche 𝐴𝐴, it reduces the effective number of ecological gene loci from three to two, thereby weakening disruptive ecological selection. Consequently, invasion of the CI eliminates the preestablished fixed-point polymorphism in the 𝐴𝐴𝑝𝑝/𝐵𝐵𝑝𝑝 phase portrait and drives the population vector to the origin, where the 𝑃𝑃 allele is lost.

Fig 53a. In a model with three incompatibility gene loci, the 𝑨𝑨𝑷𝑷/𝑩𝑩𝑷𝑷 phase portrait fails to converge without sufficiently strong disruptive ecological selection and hybrid incompatibility selection. With 𝑣𝑣 = 0.3 and 𝑓𝑓 = 0.1, the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait is divergent, even though these same parameter values yield convergence in the corresponding one-locus-equivalent two-allele model shown in Fig 46d. This difference arises because the two models are not directly comparable: in Fig 46d, 𝑣𝑣 is defined assuming no viable incompatibility hybrids, whereas in the multilocus model, hybrid genotypes are eliminated via a V-shaped fitness function parameterized by 𝑣𝑣. In the current model, the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait remains divergent for 𝑣𝑣 = 0.3 and 𝑓𝑓 = 0.1 when the number of incompatibility loci is fewer than five.

Fig 22. Increasing the number of ecological gene loci strengthens premating RI. As shown, increasing the number of gene loci for the ecological genotype in Fig 8 from three to four, while keeping all other parameter values unchanged during the pre-CI phase, shifts the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 fixed point closer to the lower-right corner of the phase portrait, indicating greater assortment of mating-bias alleles between the niche ecotypes and stronger RI. The final pre-CI total inter-niche offspring ratio (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶) decreases from 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 0.59204 in Fig 8 to 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 0.54875, reflecting reduced interniche mating and enhanced premating RI.

Fig 21. Eliminating viable ecological hybrids strengthens premating RI. Selecting the Extreme option for Habitat Resource Distribution in Fig 8, while keeping all other parameter values unchanged during the pre-CI phase of the simulation, removes all hybrid niche resources. As a result, compared with Fig 8, the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 fixed point shifts closer to the lower-right corner of the phase portrait, indicating stronger RI. The final pre-CI total inter-niche offspring ratio (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶) decreases from 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 0.59204 in Fig 8 to 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 = 0.30769 after the elimination of viable hybrids, reflecting reduced inter-niche mating and enhanced premating RI between the niche ecotypes.

Fig 54b. Genotype distribution at the fixed point in a model with three incompatibility gene loci. The pre-CI bar graph shows the genotype distribution at the fixed point of the 𝐴𝐴𝑃𝑃/𝐵𝐵𝑃𝑃 phase portrait in Fig 54a.

Fig 29b. Bar graph showing the post-CI genotype distribution after the invasion of a CI (CI = 𝒂𝒂𝒂𝒂𝒂𝒂𝑿𝑿) when 𝑵𝑵𝑨𝑨 < 𝑵𝑵𝑩𝑩 in a system with three-locus ecological genotypes. The post-CI genotype distribution corresponding to Fig 29a shows that the more numerous 𝑌𝑌 alleles in niche 𝐵𝐵 cannot eliminate the 𝑋𝑋 allele in niche 𝐴𝐴 because 𝑋𝑋 is linked to the niche-𝐴𝐴 ecotype within the CI and cannot be removed from niche 𝐴𝐴 through recombination. Consequently, the 𝑋𝑋 allele is able to hitchhike with the ecologically adaptive alleles in the CI and rise to near fixation in niche 𝐴𝐴. However, the CI invasion also increases the 𝑋𝑋 allele ratio in niche 𝐵𝐵, resulting in little net change in premating RI, with 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 remaining nearly identical after the invasion. Post-CI 𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 decreases because fewer hybrid offspring are produced in inter-niche heterokaryotype matings.

Fig 28. In a system with three-locus ecological genotypes, when 𝑵𝑵𝑨𝑨 > 𝑵𝑵𝑩𝑩, invasion of a CI capturing all adaptive ecological alleles and the prevalent mating-bias allele 𝑿𝑿 in niche 𝑨𝑨 causes the system to become divergent. Here, the Habitat Resource Distribution in Fig 26 (the 80% option with equal 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴:𝑁𝑁𝐵𝐵 ratios of 0.4:0.4) is replaced by the distribution shown in the left table of Fig 27, where 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 > 𝑁𝑁𝐵𝐵 (0.6: 0.2). The resulting single-line plot shows that, following the invasion of the CI (CI = 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑋𝑋), the 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁 fixed point is eliminated, and the 𝑌𝑌 mating-bias allele is lost after the post-CI phase of the simulation.

Fig 7. Representative phase portrait showing the population dynamics of a two-mating-bias-allele, twoniche model developed in a prior study. Given the parameter values 𝑁𝑁𝐴𝐴 = 0.6, α = 0.3, 𝑓𝑓 = 0.125, and 𝑛𝑛 = 4, the phase portrait’s vector field converges after 𝑔𝑔 = 100 generations to fixed-point polymorphisms at 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁1/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁1 = 0.94002/0.09567 and 𝐴𝐴𝑁𝑁2/𝐵𝐵𝑁𝑁2 = 0.05998/0.90433, establishing premating RI between niche 𝐴𝐴 and niche 𝐵𝐵 ecotypes. In this case, the system is said to be “convergent” (i.e., its phase portrait converges to fixed points), in contrast to a “divergent” system, where the phase portrait does not converge to fixed points.
